data.title: BW LPG Could Support A Double-Digit Yield Even With Rates Under Pressure
data.description:
I have covered several companies in the shipping industry over the years, but BW LPG Limited (NYSE:BWLP) never managed to draw my attention. But its case appears quite interesting to me today because very large gas carrier rates have cooled, and the stock has followed suit. Investors are probably wondering if the huge dividends they saw in the previous two years will ever return. And yet, it seems that even with rates under pressure, the math still works, and a rebound wouldn’t need much to push the yield to the double digits.

The Dividend Setup

BW LPG has historically paid massive dividends. Its dividend policy changes in line with leverage, with a payout of 50–100% of shipping NPAT. In any case, dividends end up being quite high even during sub-optimal market conditions. To its credit, since its IPO, the company has paid out about 3/4 of its earnings as dividends.

BW Group, BWLP's holding company, is the largest owner, holding roughly 30% of the shares. Their incentives have therefore historically aligned with common holders income-wise.

Impressively, in the up-cycle (2023), BWLP paid $3.46/share in dividends. That was a 23% annualized yield off the period-end share price. Last year, it still paid a hefty $2.42/share even as earnings declined, marking a 21% annualized yield off the year-end share price.

This year, the market’s softer rates are evident in the gradual drop of quarterly payouts. They came in at $0.28 for Q1 and an even lower $0.22 for Q2, on EPS of $0.30 and $0.23, respectively.

Rates Are Softer, But The Math Still Works

Last quarter, BW LPG posted VLGC TCE income of $38,800/available day, down from $49,700/day the year before, which, as I just mentioned, made its mark in the underlying hit to EPS and DPS. Note that the company did say decent time-charter cover supporting earnings, but the overall direction QoQ has been down.

You can see how fast earnings react when the U.S.–Far East LPG arbitrage narrows (less cargo margin to share with shipping) or Panama Canal dynamics shift. When that arb narrowed earlier in the year, spot VLGC rates came under pressure. If they widen again, ton-mile demand and rates should go back up.

Now, where does that leave us today? Well, annualizing the most recent $0.22 quarterly dividend against BWLP’s ~$13 share price implies a forward yield around the mid-6% range. That's nowhere near the monster yields from a couple of years ago.

However, as you can see in BW LPG's latest presentation, utilizing this year's fixed-out positions and a cash breakeven in the mid-$24,000s/day, the company could sustain a high single-digit yield of around $50k/day and a low double-digit yield of about 11%, around $60k/day. I should mention that the stock price management used at the time the graph was made (as per the presentation's notes) was around $12.50 (in Aug 28). Therefore, a double-digit yield at today’s levels seems possible, even close to $50k/day, which is around current levels.

And, if rates were to start to move back up, a yield above 15%-20% at today's share price cost shouldn't be that tough to achieve. Continued U.S. export growth, with capacity additions from the second half of this year, could support volumes. In turn, vessel demand could be a positive driver for rates. And clearly, more barrels and longer voyages should lead to higher TCEs. Also, note that the widening between BLPG3 (U.S.–Japan) and BLPG1 (MEG–Japan) as U.S. policy changes push vessel redeployments. This may also tighten the effective supply and support rates.

What Could Go Wrong, And What It’s Worth

What I fear the most about BWLP's case is that VLGC order book because it sits north of 100 ships (with 2027 being particularly heavy), and deliveries can cap rallies if demand doesn’t keep the same or faster pace. Moreover, if the U.S.–Asia arb stays tight, we might see a repeat of the Q2 pattern, with healthy utilization but then again less pricing power.

Valuation-wise, I don't have much to comment on. BWL is essentially trading close to its book value. Whether you believe it should trade at 0.9x, 1.1x, or 1.2x has to do with your personal projection of how high or low rates (and thus, earnings) can move from here.

I, personally, feel that rates will find support at today's levels and could even move higher if the order book setup doesn't eat much of the incoming growth in demand. Along with the potential for a double-digit dividend yield at current rates or modestly higher rates, I view BWLP as more or less fairly valued.

Final Thoughts

Look, rates are softer, and the recent trend in dividends reflects that. Still, BWLP benefits from a low cash break-even point. The very clear payout policy is also a plus, and I don't see its largest shareholder stopping from continuing to prioritize hefty payouts over the long run.

At today’s price, the forward yield sits around 6.7% based on the most recent quarterly payment. Then again, BWLP's sensitivity chart shows potential for a double-digit yield at today's share price, especially if TCE climbs back into the $60k/day area. And with seasonal tailwinds and structural export growth, I like the odds that the distribution trend goes up from here.

data.title: Undercovered Dozen: Petrobras, Richtech Robotics, Enbridge And More
data.description:
The Undercovered Dozen is a weekly Seeking Alpha editor-curated series highlighting 12 articles on lesser-covered stocks from the previous seven days. We hope this provides ideas and inspires discussion among the community.

Today, we're looking at articles published between Oct. 3 and Oct. 9.

Take a look at what these less-covered stocks might hold for you. And please join the conversation below to share what you think: Are any of these worth following up on? And are there other undercovered ideas that you like?

Petrobras: Buy This Gigantic Yield

Jonathan Weber | Buy

Petrobras, or Petroleo Brasileiro (NYSE:PBR)(NYSE:PBR.A), is an energy company that offers an attractive combination of growth potential, high-quality assets, a very low valuation, and a very attractive dividend -- the forward dividend yield is in the double-digits, and that doesn't even require a high payout ratio. While politics and country dependency on Brazil are factors to keep in mind, I think that the potential rewards are worth the risk when it comes to an investment in Petrobras.

Read more here...

Richtech Robotics: A Billion-Dollar Company With $3 Million In Sales

Julia Ostian | Hold

Richtech Robotics (NASDAQ:RR) stock has grown over 800% in the past year. It’s a small robotics company that grew to over one billion dollars in valuation due to this surge.

At its core, Richtech is trying to become the “Robot-as-a-Service” company, or RaaS, the same way the software evolved into SaaS a long time ago. They want to rent out their robots instead of just selling them to customers and make recurring revenue from subscriptions and support for these robots. This is an amazing goal that everyone should aim for, although, of course, there is a slight problem. They are far from the number one player in robotics, but the valuation is years ahead of the fundamentals.

Read more here...

C3.ai: Be Greedy When Others Are Fearful

Bay Area Ideas | Buy

While most AI names have continued their impressive bull run in the past year, C3.ai, Inc. (NYSE:AI) is not one of them. Despite its ticker being 'AI,' investors haven't demonstrated irrational exuberance. In fact, the stock is down around 20% in the past 52 weeks. From this poor performance, it can be inferred that the financial results of the company are in a less than desirable position currently. Today, we'll conduct an analysis to see if there is an opportunity for a turnaround.

In the below analysis, it is shown that the most recent financial results were poor. While investors were spooked, the weakness was really because of reorganization and Founder Siebel's poor health. Moving forward, these are likely to be very short-term issues. Furthermore, the company is seeing adoption of their AI solutions, and the company is pushing into areas like agentic AI. With the growth story secure, the recent valuation contraction seems like an overreaction that provides great long term opportunity. As a result, I have decided to initiate C3.ai at a buy rating.

Read more here...

NANO Nuclear Energy Can't Stop Running

Jack Bowman | Buy

I last covered NANO Nuclear Energy Inc. (NASDAQ:NNE) back in July, after a large run-up that I said would hold up due to the abundance of catalysts and the positive market and regulatory environments for small-scale nuclear power firms, especially the speculative, pre-revenue firms like NNE.

What I didn't know then is that I'd be prompted to re-evaluate my position by October already, following a 20% jump in the share price.

Read more here...

Enbridge: The Indirect AI Winner

Dair Sansyzbayev | Strong Buy

My bullish call on Enbridge (NYSE:ENB) back in March has worked out well. The stock has returned around 23% since then, beating the broader U.S. stock market. For an energy infrastructure company with steady cash flows, that’s an impressive performance.

Despite global macroeconomic uncertainty, I remain extremely bullish on ENB. This is a company with rock-solid fundamentals and a unique position in the North American midstream industry. Furthermore, unlike oil producers whose profits are tied to the rise and fall of crude prices, ENB earns the bulk of its revenue from transporting and processing energy. And this is a setup that generates a highly stable cash flow. Another reason for my bullish stance is the current AI revolution. There’s no denying that running data centers sucks up an enormous amount of power, much of which is still sourced from natural-gas-fired plants. As ENB owns and operates a massive natural gas pipeline, it is in the perfect position to benefit from the AI wave. Finally, ENB’s valuation remains highly attractive. Add in the steady cash flow, solid dividends, and AI-driven demand, I believe this company is a compelling investment opportunity. All in all, I am reiterating my "Strong Buy" rating.

Read more here...

PLTW: Let Palantir Pay Your Weekly Bills

Binary Tree Analytics | Buy

Single-name ETFs have become very popular in the past few years, with derivatives financial engineering being layered on top of classic equities in order to extract dividends. It is the case for Roundhill PLTR WeeklyPay ETF (BATS:PLTW), an exchange-traded fund launched in February 2025. ...

In today's article we are going to have a closer look at PLTW and its structure, look at its analytics and performance, and articulate our view on this innovative ETF.

Read more here...

USA Compression: Leveraging Energy Transfer For Long-Term Growth

Ronald Ferrie | Buy

USA Compression (NYSE:USAC) is making incremental business improvements that also coincide with a slight downward trend in the company’s unit price. These improvements make the company more likely to achieve positive FCF in 2026 while also improving the company’s yield to over 9%.

Investors can reap the benefits of this yield while the natural gas compression market continues to expand. This driver, as well as the company’s unique relationship with Energy Transfer (ET), makes for a powerful combination. Now that the two companies have entered into a shared services agreement, USAC is positioned to ride on the tail of ET’s growth plans.

I continue to rate USAC as a BUY.

Read more here...

Teva Pharmaceutical: Turnaround Driven By Huge Pipeline Upside

Bondy Investing | Buy

Teva Pharmaceutical Industries Limited (NYSE:TEVA) (OTCPK:TEVJF) is a pharmaceutical company specializing in generics and biosimilars. The company has been a disappointment for investors due to several issues, including a heavy debt load and legal issues regarding opioids. However, I believe it's time for investors to re-examine this stock, as the company's restructuring plan is leading to improved margins and its pipelines, especially Duvakitug, have huge upside potential.

Read more here...

DexCom May Be The Most Underrated Growth Story In Healthcare

Datalam Trading | Buy

I treat DexCom (NASDAQ:DXCM) as defensive growth. It can still put up numbers if the cycle turns. The company is delivering relatively interesting figures and has also raised its outlook for 2025. I was also intrigued by the fact that it is opening up a new market outside its main area of activity with insulin. That's why I dug in.

DXCM grew to $1.157 billion in revenue in Q2 2025 (YoY +15%) and raised its outlook for 2025 to $4.6 billion–$4.625 billion, with the goal of maintaining a gross margin of 62%. A secondary goal is to maintain an operating margin of 21% and an EBITDA margin of 30%. I like that this is not just a vision, but a real execution that can be verified in the financial statements.

Read more here...

ImmunityBio: A Small Bet Is Warranted

Busted IPO Forum | Buy

Shares of immunotherapy and vaccine concern ImmunityBio, Inc. (NASDAQ:IBRX) are down nearly two-thirds over the past 18 months as cash needs forced dilution to the tune of 37%. Its IL-15 super-agonist fusion protein Anktiva is beginning to ramp meaningfully now that it has received a J-code but faces a threat from TAR-200, which received a priority review on July 31st. With a potential pipeline in a product with Anktiva, a cash burn not likely to end for three more years, a promising t-haNK program, and increasing Street support, ImmunityBio merited a deeper dive.

Read more here...

Vertiv Technologies: 2025's AI Breakout Facing A Cooldown?

Undercovered Deep Insights | Hold

I covered Vertiv Technologies (NYSE:VRT) in Seeking Alpha’s big 2025 stock-pick competition in December last year.

I didn’t win the contest, but the stock definitely delivered!

Since covering the Vertiv in my article titled “Vertiv’s AI-Driven Growth: A Winning Stock for 2025,” the stock has skyrocketed over 30% and is now within touching distance of my fair value estimate of around $167 at the time.

However, despite the stock looking overstretched, its underlying fundamentals continue to shine.

Vertiv recently delivered another top-and-bottom-line beat in Q2 and continues to execute superbly. Q2 2025 sales came in at $2.64 billion (up 35% year-over-year), while its EPS of 95 cents surged 42%.

Also, it posted its first-ever quarter, delivering over $3 billion in quarterly orders backed by an $8.5 billion backlog. Consequently, Vertiv pushed its 2025 guidance in Q2 by a sizeable margin across both lines. Clearly, a huge part of that comes from its role in hyperscale giants like Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), Google (GOOGL), and Meta Platforms (META).

Despite the stellar execution, though, the stock’s clearly overheated.

From March 31 to late September, VRT went parabolic, jumping 206% and now trades at 77 times GAAP (TTM) earnings. That prompted me to run a refreshed DCF (15% FCF growth, 8% WACC), pegging an updated fair value for VRT stock at $155, implying mid-teens downside from current levels.

From a technical standpoint, though, the uptrend is intact, and $165–$170 on strong volume will likely extend upside. However, in the absence of a breakout, it makes more sense to trim some gains, keep a core position, and continue adding again on pullbacks ($150–$145).

Long term, though, there’s plenty to like about Vertiv as a premier AI-infrastructure play, but it’s just hot to the touch right now.

Read more here...

You Can't Fight Facts. Raising AOD To A Strong Buy While Lowering AGD To A Sell

Douglas Albo | Buy, Sell

OK, this is my third article addressing what is an unstoppable eventuality, since the fundamentals have actually worsened for one fund while making another fund that much more attractive.

What am I talking about? Two equity based CEFs from Aberdeen, the abrdn Total Dynamic Dividend Fund (NYSE:AOD), $9.55 closing market price, and the abrdn Global Dynamic Dividend Fund (NYSE:AGD), $13.57 closing market price, may seem like two different funds in that AGD has Global in its name.

But in actuality, they are virtually the same. You can go up and down the two funds' holdings and the only difference is that AOD owns roughly 3.5X more shares of each position because AOD is roughly 3.5X the size of its much smaller sister fund, AGD.

Read more here...

data.title: These Statistics Are A Real Problem For The Bears
data.description:
Another down day for the major market indexes, but not before the S&P 500 set a new intraday record. Earnings season got off to a good start with better-than-expected numbers from Delta and PepsiCo, but more conjecturing by pundits about a weakening economy and bubbles brewing in AI land kept risk assets under pressure. After a 36% surge in the S&P 500 from the April correction low, it is natural for even the most bullish of investors to be cautious, but new all-time highs have historically not limited the prospects for the market moving forward. Further emboldening my bullish outlook, I share three sets of statistics that suggest there are more gains ahead.

I have been an ardent bull on the economy and stock market for more than three years now, but I tempered my enthusiasm temporarily this summer after the president announced his trade and tariff policies. I expected a pullback at best and a correction at worst to factor in what I viewed as significant headwinds to the ongoing expansion, but neither materialized. Perhaps I should have listened to the market more closely, as the more than 25% gain that the S&P 500 posted over three months, following its April low, had a wildly bullish precedent. That was only its sixth occurrence since 1950, and in the previous five, the index rose over the following three months every time by an average of 8.8%. Here we are three months later, and it is up 7.2%. Guess what happened over the six-month and one-year period that followed?

As we moved into September, following the resumption of rate cuts by the Fed, the historical precedent grew even stronger for more bull market gains. When the Fed has cut rates at a time when the S&P 500 was within 2% of its all-time high in data going back to 1980, the index was higher one year later all 20 times. Combine this statistic with the one I just reviewed, and the bulls have two powerful tailwinds through September of next year.

Now for the icing on the bull market’s cake. We just completed a six-month period, following the April low, during which the S&P 500 has risen more than 35%, which is only its sixth occurrence since 1950. The index was higher 12 months later after all prior occurrences. This takes us out to October of next year, which, in combination with the precedent for rate cuts by the Fed within 2% of an all-time high, is a very powerful bullish precedent.

Can we have a pullback or correction over the coming year? Absolutely! It would be abnormal to not see one, but I suspect it will be shallow and short-lived. While the basis for my bullish outlook on the market and the economy is fundamentally based, these quantitative statistics suggest I am on the right path.

data.title: Nissan: Re:Nissan Needs To Succeed
data.description:
Nissan Motor Co., Ltd. (OTCPK:NSANY) is facing issues. The large Japanese automaker has failed to attract buyers with its car models, forcing the company to go into deep restructuring to maintain profitability. No success is guaranteed as Nissan’s sales continue to lag – the company’s Re:Nissan turnaround strategy still needs to drive clear improvements going forward.

The stock’s performance reflects Nissan’s issues; Nissan’s stock has lost three quarters of its value during the past decade. Without many positives to note so far, the investment has continued to perform poorly. Nissan doesn’t currently pay out a dividend, reflecting the company’s weak financial position.

Nissan’s Earnings Are in Trouble

Nissan is in deep trouble. The company’s car models have failed to attract consumers, setting Nissan up for financial trouble. Nissan’s sales have varied in past years, and operating profits have plummeted into a -¥10 billion (around $67 million) loss from the past twelve months at a slightly negative -0.1% margin. The latest fiscal Q1 report showed a sharpening -9.7% revenue drop and a -¥79 billion operating loss, continuing Nissan’s troubling financial performance at an accelerating pace.

The Nissan Leaf EV hasn’t been able to compete effectively with Tesla (TSLA), Hyundai, and other EV competitors, while Nissan’s older core models haven’t been updated to compete effectively with Toyota (TM) and others. Nissan’s brand has lacked direction. The company consequently recently fell from the list of top ten selling automakers.

Debt makes Nissan’s situation worse; the company holds ¥6.87 trillion in interest-bearing debts on its balance sheet. While Nissan’s interest expenses aren’t very high, the debt ultimately has to be paid off or refinanced, potentially causing trouble given the current earnings profile. Making Nissan’s position worse, Nissan’s free cash flow stands at -¥2.74 trillion over the past twelve months – high investments and weak operating earnings have made the company’s financial situation dire. The company is clearly in urgent need of a turnaround.

The Re:Nissan Strategy Right-Sizes Nissan, But Success Remains to Be Proven

Nissan has clearly acknowledged its issues and is working to fix them; Ivan Espinosa took over as CEO in early 2025 to drive a turnaround. The Re:Nissan plan was unveiled in May, looking to overhaul Nissan’s business through the next few years.

The most notable part is Nissan’s significant restructuring to fix the cost base – Nissan is consolidating its manufacturing footprint from 17 plants to only 10, reducing workforce by a total of 20000 across manufacturing, SG&A, and R&D functions, and reducing other variable and fixed costs across the organization. Nissan isn’t trying to aggressively recapture lost sales but rather rightsizing the business to maintain profitability with lower sales.

To maintain still healthy sales, Nissan is reorganizing the company’s market and product strategy. Nissan looks to create models on a more regionally focused basis, dividing its segments to the U.S., Japan, China, Europe, Middle East, and Mexico. The company is also prioritizing partnerships in many markets to create regionally relevant models. Representatively of the strategy, Nissan is now reportedly negotiating a partnership with Ford (F) and Stellantis (STLA) to bring a hybrid SUV to the U.S. market. The third-generation Nissan Leaf launch adds to Nissan’s product efforts.

Ultimately, Nissan’s turnaround efforts are still in early stages; not much should be expected from Nissan’s upcoming quarters yet. While restructuring should aid maintain profitability, I believe that recapturing consumer interest is ultimately the most critical factor in Nissan’s long-term turnaround. Global Nissan sales volume did show healthier 2.8% growth in August, but consistently improved sales momentum remains to be proven.

Valuation

I built a discounted cash flow (DCF) model to estimate a fair value for Nissan’s stock. I estimate slow growth as a base scenario – as Nissan consolidates its manufacturing, the company isn’t setting itself up for much topline growth. I estimate revenue growth to stabilize at only a 0.5% annual level over the mid-term.

The Re:Nissan strategy should drive margin gains if sales do stabilize. I assume the EBIT margin to stabilize at 4.5% over the mid-term as a base scenario, improving significantly from FY2025 but not faring to most automakers’ margins such as Volkswagen’s (OTCPK:VWAGY) 6.3% EBIT margin and Toyota’s 9.6% EBIT margin. The cash flow conversion is likely to be worsened by heightened EV investments, but I assume that Nissan will return to fairly healthy cash flow regardless with improving EBIT.

The estimates put Nissan’s fair value at $4.4, 11% below the stock price at the time of writing; the stock still doesn’t seem to be very attractive at the current price. A full turnaround could make the stock very attractive especially as high debt makes Nissan’s valuation volatile, but I believe that such a scenario shouldn’t be priced in yet. For now, the valuation is roughly fair, but is likely to see much turbulence ahead.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 6.16 %, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a low 1.15% interest rate, annualized from Nissan’s Q1 interest expenses and debt. I assume the long-term debt-to-equity ratio to be high at 80% as Nissan’s equity value is very low.

For cost of equity, I use Japan’s 10-year bond yield of 1.70% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 5.25% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent July estimate for Japan. I use Aswath Damodaran’s 1.62 beta estimate for the auto/truck industry. With a liquidity premium of 0.3%, the cost of equity stands at 10.41% and the WACC at 6.16%.

Takeaway

Nissan has struggled in maintaining consumer interest with its car models, and the company’s financial performance clearly reflects the fact. The company is now focused on a turnaround with the Re:Nissan strategy, rightsizing the company and reorganizing the market and product strategy. Success remains to be proven – Nissan will likely be a very volatile investment. I initiate NSANY stock at a Hold rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: From The Discount Rack To Your Portfolio: Two 8%+ Yielders
data.description:
The risk of major correction is growing day by day.

On the one hand, we have equity markets (SPY) setting record highs and credit spreads flirting with 10-year lows.

On the other hand, the risks in the underlying economy are not only increasing, but have already started to materialize. Almost in every major economic data category there are some issues. For example, the inflation remains stubbornly high. The labor market shows clear signs of weakness. The private credit space (BIZD) has started to tumble. Corporate bankruptcies are on a rise. The non-AI CapEx spend is depressed.

It is obviously a very subjective topic, but, in my humble opinion, the probabilities for experiencing notable pullback are greater than enjoying additional gains.

At the same time, we (those, who believe more in the former scenario) have to be realistic and appreciate the fact that such kind of thinking has historically turned out to be quite expensive. Namely, there is a significant opportunity cost that could stem from sitting on sidelines or playing extra safe - e.g., investing in quality bonds (BND), top-quality CLOs (JAAA), T-Bills (TBIL) etc.

The question is how to navigate this?

I think that investing in high-yield assets that have sufficient margin of safety could be an optimal route to take.

If investors can ensure that they lock in attractive dividends that are underpinned by robust fundamentals, then at least the yield component could serve as a "guaranteed" return in plunging markets. The key is to have long-term investment horizon (to wait until prices recover, because high and quality yield do not mean that share price won't fall) and really strong financials for the companies to preserve their dividends across different economic cycles.

Another element to focus on when thinking about margin of safety is the valuation multiples. The bigger the daylight between company valuations and how other peers are priced (including the company's historical valuation levels), the more protected we are in case of a system-wide correction.

In other words, if we have steep discounts in place, then the downside is protected by the following two things:

  1. Quality high-yield (i.e., hard to imagine investment grade blue-chip suddenly yielding, say, 15% due to macro related factors).
  2. Already depressed valuation multiples.

Let me now present you two concrete investment examples that quality in the abovementioned framework.

#1 Energy Transfer - 7.9%

Energy Transfer (NYSE:ET) is a large-cap midstream operator with one of the strongest market positions in the natural gas space. It owns more than 105,000 miles of pipes moving ~30% of the U.S. total natgas production.

This something, where such peers as Enterprise Products Partners L.P. (NYSE:EPD), Plains All American Pipeline, L.P. (NYSE:PAA), MPLX LP (NYSE:MPLX), and Western Midstream (NYSE:WES) cannot outcompete ET. And the most important thing here is that it is exactly the natural gas part of midstream business that has strong growth prospects, while traditional oil segments are slowly but surely losing traction. The key driver is obviously the AI and the need for power to accommodate data centers, where reliable baseload energy is a must (like natural gas).

If we compare ET's valuation multiple (EV/EBITDA) to the four peers that I mentioned above, we will notice a tangible difference. Currently, ET trades at EV/EBITDA of 8.3x. Meanwhile, EPD, PAA, MPLX and WES can be bought at 10.2x, 8.9x, 10.0x and 8.8x, respectively.

Theoretically, this could be explained by the fact that ET has cut its distributions in 2021 (e.g., EPD and MPLX avoided it), which in combination with ~4.0x leverage does not sound too good.

Yet, I think that the notion of ET being aggressively leveraged and thus with an unsustainable yield (value) lacks meaningful basis.

Here is why.

First, since 2021 ET has completely transformed its business, making it more diversified, focused on natural gas and biased towards fee-based cash flows.

Second, about 90% of cash flows are tied to cost-of-service like contracts, which come with annual CPI escalators, long duration profiles and strong (IG grade) counterparties.

Third, as of Q2, 2025, the DCF payout stood at 76%, which means that annually ET can retain about $2 billion of pure cash in the system that can be directed towards growth and/or balance sheet optimization. In my view, there is no major need to do the latter because the capital structure is already at the investment grade level.

Fourth, about half of 2025 CapEx amount is related to natural gas and as we move into 2026, it is very likely that we will see this share increasing. Becoming even more natural gas focused player should provide stronger basis for not only healthy earnings growth, but also for a potential repricing (at least closer to the sector average).

In a nutshell, ET is clearly discounted despite its very robust fundamentals and solid growth prospects. The attractive yield coupled with margin of safety on dividend and balance sheet front makes ET a timely investment in the prevailing market situation.

#2 Morgan Stanley Direct Lending - 12.1%

Morgan Stanley Direct Lending Fund (NYSE:MSDL) presents a completely different story, but the underlying substance remains the same - i.e., undervalued, high-yield and strong fundamentals.

Looking at the ~12.1% yield we can already conclude that the risks are way higher here than in ET's case. MSDL is a BDC, which is makes money from private credit lending. It is not only that BDC exposure brings more risks, but also the current market environment bodes ill for the sector. Namely, the interest rates are falling, spreads compressing and fears of private credit distress rising.

On top of that, MSDL's current dividend yield is far from safe. The NII per share just barely covers the quarterly base dividend. Now, if we couple this with the following three items, the picture will go from bad to worse:

  1. Consistently declining NII per share results over the past couple of quarters.
  2. About 50 bps to 100 bps still to go in terms of portfolio-wide spread compression.
  3. Already 25 bps of a cut that has not yet been reflected in the financials (I'm not even speaking about additional cuts).

But just to finish the topic of interest rate cuts, based on Q2, 2025 10-Q filing, we can see that a 25 bps interest rate reduction would result in about 10% of a decline in the NII per share generation. 100 bps cut would trigger about 30% of a decline in the NII per share.

Having said that, the way how I look at MSDL's case is what yield we get after the cut and whether the post-cut yield will be sustainable as we move forwards.

My base case assumption is that MSDL slashes the dividend by 20% to 25%, which would leave us with a yield of about 9.5% - i.e., still a material current income level. The reason why I am not assuming fully synchronized dividend reduction with the 100 bps interest rate cut scenario is because a) it is still unclear whether 100 bps is realistic in the near to medium-term and b) MSDL has above average fundamentals that can drive incremental top-line growth, which would then offset at least part of the negatives stemming from lower interest rate environment.

For example, MSDL has a decent chunk of outstanding debt at fixed rates that are above market levels. The most recent BDC fixed rate debt issuances signal that a range of 5.2% to 5.4% is achievable for Baa3 rated BDCs. Let's not forget that the NII per share interest rate sensitivity assumptions that I indicated above are based on the current debt mix, which we already now will very likely transform into something more positive.

Plus, MSDL carries a solid balance sheet with debt to equity of 1.15x. This level indicates that MSDL has some spare debt capacity in tank to capitalize on lower interest rate environment, which is inherently more accommodative for increased M&A and LBO activity. And the larger the loan book, the larger the base from which to capture spreads and thus offset the forthcoming negatives.

Finally, the thing that we have to also really appreciate is MSDL's P/NAV metric. Currently, it indicates that MSDL is trading at 20% discount to NAV, which is even below the sector average of 0.87x. Given MSDL's earnings quality, below average non-accruals, focus on non-cyclical investments and solid balance sheet, I simply do not see a logical explanation for the current disconnect. I'm not arguing about the fact that MSDL is discounted relative to the NAV, but what I'm saying is that it does not make sense that MSDL - as high-quality BDC - trades significantly below sector average, which is comprised of many weaker BDCs.

In summary, MSDL offers a high-yield case, which after a dividend cut should still provide very attractive current income stream level that is supported by high-quality fundamentals (and decent discount from which to enjoy recovery).

data.title: Affirm: The Only Overvalued Stock I'm Buying
data.description:
Introduction

Affirm Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:AFRM) is not the first buy-now-pay-later ("BNPL") financial technology company that I am breaking down on Seeking Alpha. As a matter of fact, I broke down Klarna’s (KLAR) IPO just a month ago, and I gave it a Hold rating, saying that it was another IPO to avoid. And now, just yesterday, we got the news that both Affirm and Klarna have entered into collaborations with Alphabet/Google (GOOG, GOOGL, GOOG:CA), which is obviously great news for both companies. Klarna was up about 4%, and Affirm was up more than 5%.

So naturally, after this kind of move, everyone is curious: Is it still a good time to buy Affirm stock? Because, first of all, the stock of this company had been on a nonstop rally from the beginning of April until almost the end of September. Then it pulled back significantly, from around $90 per share down to $73. But now, after the surge that we just witnessed, is it a good time to buy Affirm stock?

Numbers

If we open the latest earnings presentation from Affirm, we can see that the company is in great shape and growing at an incredible pace. Looking at all the key metrics like gross merchandise volume, active customers, transactions per active customer, and revenue, they’re all up significantly.

This is the reason why the stock is so expensive. When you get growth like this, you usually pay a premium for it. Gross merchandise volume has grown by 43%, and active consumers are up by 24% year over year. What surprised me even more is that transactions per active customer (basically internal growth) increased by 19%. This means the company isn’t just growing through new customer acquisition but also because existing customers are using Affirm much more frequently. That’s impressive, because acquiring new customers always comes with a cost, while increasing engagement from existing ones points to real efficiency.

I know that people often critique the buy-now-pay-later system because, of course, we could argue that it’s always better to use your own money and spend only what you can afford, not beyond that. I have never taken debt, and as a matter of fact, never been in the minus on my credit card, so believe me, you don’t need to convince me. But at the same time, we can’t ignore the fact that this is simply how most people live, especially in developed countries.

If we look at the total debt balance and its composition from 2003 up to 2023, we can see it’s been growing steadily. I assume it will continue to grow over the years. With this assumption, we can expect that the number of people who get used to living beyond their means (spending more than they actually can) will keep increasing. Again, we can debate whether it’s right or wrong, but that’s not related to Affirm and our investment decisions in this case.

In general, if we imagine a world with no debt at all (where it’s impossible to borrow money), I would argue that the U.S. wouldn’t be the strongest economy. The first time I heard about people living heavily in debt was at my university here in Israel (where I live), and I was shocked. In my economics class, they mentioned that around 70–80% of households in Israel live with debt. That sounded insane to me because I’m originally from Ukraine, where debt has always been viewed as something impossible or even shameful to have.

After I thought about that, I figured maybe it has something to do with stability. If you live in a growing, stable economy, you’re not afraid to take on debt because you trust that interest rates won’t suddenly jump to 30% overnight. I was very curious about it, and at the same time, it didn’t feel right. But if you dig deeper, you’ll see that most of the highly leveraged people and countries are actually the most successful and the largest economies overall. Now that I know a little more than in my first economics course, I am sure I will be highly leveraged in my investment in a few years, when I’ll try out different strategies and will pick the best-fitted one for that debt to make me money.

So again, whether it’s right or wrong can be debated endlessly, but one thing I’m sure about, or at least assume, is that debt will continue to increase over time. And if we take that into account, we can see that Affirm not only grows within its own sector but also benefits from the overall sector growth. So it’s a double positive.

Besides that, Affirm was founded by Max Levchin, one of the co-founders of PayPal (PYPL), which is already a huge advantage. And he’s also Ukrainian, so, of course, it helped me warm up to this company after I learned the fact.

Company Overview

Affirm’s network is starting to look like a real marketplace, which is interesting. They finished fiscal year 2025 with about 377,000 active merchants, and what I like more than the top-line count is the behavior on the other side of the platform. Transaction frequency increased about 20%, which tells us people are building a habit of using Affirm, and they definitely enjoy the experience. Here we get the snowball law working: more places to use Affirm means more reasons for consumers to sign up, which, in turn, brings more merchants.

Affirm was in general created to solve the “$500 problem.” It is something that’s often mentioned in the fintech and retail industry to describe a hesitation that happens in consumer behavior. In simple terms, research over the years (including reports by the Federal Reserve) shows that many Americans find it difficult to handle an unexpected $400 or $500 expense without borrowing or adjusting their budget. It doesn’t necessarily mean people don’t have money; it means they feel uncomfortable paying so much at one time. It’s some kind of psychological threshold.

Affirm helps people split this purchase into five payments of $100, which is much easier to afford. From the merchant’s side, this is a huge advantage, and they are paying companies for this, because the assumption is that if Affirm weren’t there, the person wouldn’t buy the product.

The great piece of news is that the company has become profitable. Management promised operating profitability in Q4 2025 a year ago, which is another green flag, pointing to the competent management in making predictions and executing on time. They nearly doubled GMV (Gross Merchandise Value) from fiscal 2023 to fiscal 2025, but did not hire a lot of people, and their revenue per employee right now is at $1.5 million.

What is interesting is that Affirm is switching from being only a buy now, pay later button on the website to a card issuer for in-store purchases. Their in-store card business has been growing at an incredible pace, with GMV up 132% year over year. Besides, the number of active cardholders almost doubled, reaching around 2.3 million. So it means that people are beginning to use Affirm more like a payment method, and not just a tool to divide payments at the checkout online, which opens a whole lot of different opportunities for this company.

I am very curious where it will go after, because while this part is growing quickly, as of now, only about 10% of Affirm’s total active consumers currently have the card. That’s a very small portion, which means there’s still a huge growth runway ahead if the adoption continues, of course. It’s not for sure, but for now, it looks like it might, because Affirm continues to work in the direction of physical retail. I think this could become one of the company’s biggest growth drivers in the next few years if it really works.

Affirm’s Collaboration With Google

I noticed this company after they collaborated with Google, so I might as well address it. Yesterday, we got the news that Affirm and Klarna will partner with Google, but it looks like the deals are different for them.

Klarna’s partnership with Google Cloud will allow Klarna to expand its internal capabilities. It’s interesting, but it’s something that helps Klarna optimize its own operations rather than directly expanding and growing.

Affirm’s collaboration, on the other hand, looks more strategic in nature. The company announced its support for Google’s Agent Payments Protocol, which, according to Google, is

An open protocol developed with leading payments and technology companies to securely initiate and transact agent-led payments across platforms.

In simple words, it means that Affirm’s buy-now-pay-later option will become integrated into this architecture of “agentic commerce.” Agentic commerce is a concept where AI agents (whether it’s Google Assistant, a chatbot, or whatever else that is not human) can autonomously make purchases on behalf of users. I know it sounds weird thinking of this today, but I could see it being a big part of tomorrow’s AI assistants. What’s the use of the AI assistant if it can’t deal with your groceries, school payments for kids, flights, hotel bookings for business trips, and other things you needed an assistant for in the first place?

By collaborating with Google on this, Affirm makes sure that if this new type of commerce takes off, its presence within it will be guaranteed without heavy marketing or user acquisition spending. It sounds great, and was a smart move, although it won’t change this company overnight.

Valuation

As you could see for yourself, the company looks promising and great. But at the same time, of course, the stock is expensive. At the same time, what did you expect with 43% in GMV, 24% in active users, 20% in transaction frequency, and a newly welcomed profitability? Usually, investors pay a premium for it, especially at an all-time high market.

The challenge is that Affirm is no longer in its “hypergrowth” phase, yet the market still prices it like a high-growth company. The stock trades at a valuation that reflects strong expectations for the future, not just the current fundamentals. It’s one of those cases where you’re not buying the company for what it earns today, but for what it could earn a few years from now if it continues scaling successfully.

So yes, the multiple looks high, but the business also just turned profitable, continues to show operating leverage, and has an expanding addressable market (now both online and in-store), and potentially in agentic commerce through Google’s ecosystem. In my view, that makes the premium somewhat justified. But you have to understand that with stock like this, volatility is expected. The stock can easily go 20% in either direction without anything being “wrong.” If the market crashes, this stock will crash like an overvalued hyped name, meaning violently.

Risks

While I like the company a lot and rate it as a Buy, there are several risks worth acknowledging. The first one is credit risk. Affirm’s business depends on consumers paying their installments on time, and although rates remain relatively stable right now, a slowdown in the economy or a rise in unemployment could bring unexpected downside.

The second risk is the mix shift in its products. Management already guided that the share of 0% APR loans will increase in fiscal 2026. That’s great for customers, but it usually means a slightly lower take rate and less revenue per transaction, which could pressure margins that aren’t the best in the industry already.

Another thing to keep an eye on is merchant concentration. The company mentioned that one of its large enterprise partners may start moving some of its pay-later volumes into its own wallet by the second quarter of fiscal 2026. If that happens, it could affect GMV growth in the short term.

And finally, there’s valuation risk itself that I mentioned earlier.

The company dilutes shareholders every quarter; take that into account.

Conclusion

I rate Affirm stock as a Buy. But investors should be very cautious, because while it’s a Buy, there’s always the possibility that the stock could pull back significantly. Personally, I would buy now and gladly add if the stock goes down. If you don’t want to be a shareholder of a company that could go down and then back up again, you probably shouldn’t own it. This is not a speculative company anymore, or at least not as much as it was before profitability, but the valuation is still expensive, and it’s important to understand that.

In general, I really like Affirm. I see huge prospects for the company in the future, and I think it will grow into something much bigger than a buy-now-pay-later service over the years. It’s becoming a strong financial network. I would be holding this stock for the long term, adding right now, and possibly adding even more when it dips.

data.title: Pfizer's Q3: The Catalyst For A Bullish Resurgence (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
Updating My Thesis Ahead Of Pfizer's Q3

After numerous bullish articles that I wrote about Pfizer (NYSE:PFE) stock since February 2024, the price action is finally setting up for a solid recovery amid all the positives and recent corporate news that should theoretically support my initial bullishness. I think the bulls' case has gotten stronger now, following the recently announced 3-year reprieve on drug-import tariffs from the Trump administration, and the firm's continued investment into oncology to offset the upcoming LOIs.

The upcoming Q3 data should be released on November 4th, 2025 (in about 3 weeks), and I think the management's additional earnings commentary is going to add color on the ongoing oncology progress and future plans, as well as address Trump's administration reprieve decision. The current earnings expectations, specifically beyond 2025, seem to be too beaten down to me, and I hope to see meaningful earnings revisions after the post-Q3 commentary.

The undervaluation of the stock makes me leave my "Buy" rating with no changes.

My Updated Reasoning

After Pfizer released its Q2 and strongly beat the consensus estimates back then, the market first reacted positively, and the PFE stock price jumped slightly on the beating news. But then the momentum almost completely faded, and now the stock is trading as if there were no beating at all. Probably the main reason was the fact that most analysts decided to lower their Q3 expectations following Q2 results:

And that happened amid the management's decision to raise FY2025 EPS guidance to $2.90-$3.10 by targeting margin expansion through the initial $7.7 billion cost savings by 2027. So, the co-existence of the negative revisions and these guidance targets seems a bit odd to me.

Over the past few weeks, there were also some additional corporate events that grabbed my attention, and which should have theoretically led to a bullish repricing.

First, in late September, we found out that Pfizer "agreed to sell four of its prescription medicines, including its blockbuster arthritis drug Xeljanz, at up to an 85% discount" through TrumpRx, a new specialized website created to realize Trump's most-favored-nation (MFN) pricing policy. After that move, Pfizer also announced an additional $70 billion in U.S. manufacturing and R&D in exchange for a three-year grace period on tariffs. After these news pieces came out, the PFE stock price reacted positively, but again, the strength didn't last long. I think it's now a matter of time before the narrative turns back up again - the deal with TrumpRx and that seemingly hefty future investments are actually reducing regulatory uncertainty ahead, and they should theoretically attract new investors as soon as the firm starts to deliver on the management's guidance.

Second, Pfizer basically doubled down on its attempts to explore the highly lucrative obesity treatment market through the recently announced acquisition of Metsera.

Metsera brings its portfolio of experimental obesity drugs, with MET-097i, a GLP-1 injectable, and MET-233i, which mimics the pancreatic hormone amylin, being the key among them. MET-233i is being evaluated as a monthly monotherapy and in combination with MET-097i in early-stage trials.

Pfizer said early-stage data for MET-233i has shown a "potential best-in-class profile".

According to analysts, the easier dosing schedules could give Pfizer an edge in the market. Leerink Partners analyst David Risinger has projected more than $5 billion in combined peak sales for Metsera's pipeline.

Source: Reuters

According to MedWatch, Novo Nordisk (NVO) tried to bid higher than Pfizer's $7.3 billion offer, but "risks associated with the acquisition of Metsera played a decisive role." Anyway, the interest from the obesity treatment market's veteran tells me that Metsera's offerings may indeed be "best-in-class" as Pfizer's management noted, and if so, then the firm has effectively added a major piece to its prospective total addressable market (TAM). An addition like this is usually accompanied by subsequent market repricing, which hasn't happened just yet, as far as I can see it.

So, with the above 2 reasons, PFE's momentum should have lasted for way longer than it was the case in reality. I think this "unfairness" is going to be resolved once the market has more clarity on how the management can deliver on the updated guidance.

Now, let me turn to what's coming for PFE's upcoming Q3.

After the Q3 consensus was revised downward by almost 20% over the past 3 months, the current expectation for the quarter is that Pfizer's EPS will fall by 34.64% YoY to $0.69 amid a contraction in sales of about 4.47% YoY. From the Q2 earnings call, we know that there should be a "$0.20 charge for acquired in-process R&D associated with the upfront payment for the 3SBio transaction." So, the expected YoY fall itself isn't something to really worry about, in my understanding, as it's not a result of Pfizer's operational deterioration. And I think that the company's push for cost-cutting should partially offset the negative impact, even amid the one-time charges exepcted to take place in 2H 2025.

The odds are that Pfizer can report another strong quarter in about 3 weeks. And what can really move the needle for PFE's stock price action is the management's updated guidance. I think that the oncology business should do well, and with the recent addition of Metsera to the consolidated portfolio and the logical TAM expansion, Pfizer's valuation should get rid of its discount for the upcoming LOIs, at least to some extent.

Right now, PFE is trading at ~8.2x forward earnings multiple, which is already based on a kind of pessimistic Wall Street's EPS assumptions, but still, it's a ~39% cut to the TTM P/E multiple. With a beat to the current EPS expectation of 4-6% (last year, Pfizer beat by 6.2%), and putting a 10x earnings multiple on that, I get a price target of $33.285/share:

$3.17 * 1.05 * 10x = $33.285/share

This indicated an upside of ~32% to the current stock price.

Based on the technicals that are currently setting up, I see a potential reverse double-shoulder pattern below, and I expect that when the correction to the 200-day moving average is completed, the price can push to the nearest resistance zone shortly.

Risks And Your Takeaway

Nobody can cancel the risks of LOIs on the horizon. Here's how Argus Research's analysts put them in their analysis (proprietary source, October 2025):

Risks for Pfizer include the expiration of patents for key products, long product development cycles, and political and regulatory hurdles. In 2026-2030 period, Pfizer will face patent expirations and the loss of exclusivity for products with total peak revenue of about $17 billion.

And I may be wrong in the way I assumed the upcoming Q3 likely beat, and my subsequent valuation calculations.

But despite all that, I think that Pfizer has already been beaten down sufficiently to reflect all the possible risks that it represents. A global healthcare provider, a wide-moat pharma stock trading at less than 9x forward earnings amid improving cost control, widening TAM, and some new drugs coming to the market soon - Pfizer is one of the most obvious value opportunities out there. The recent deal with Trump's administration reduces the overall regulatory risks and should lead to a bullish repricing ahead.

Based on all that, I'm leaving my "Buy" in place, waiting for a beat to the bottom line consensus in Q3, and expecting the management's positive commentary on the earnings call.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: Innovative Solutions and Support Gains Soared 90%; Why It's Not Too Late To Buy
data.description:
Innovative Solutions and Support (NASDAQ:ISSC) is one of the aerospace companies that I have a strong buy rating on. The last time I covered the company was in December 2024. At that time, the company did not have any coverage from other Seeking Alpha analysts. Since then, the company appeared on the radar of my fellow contributors, but as a detector of aerospace gems from a stock price performance perspective, my coverage has had an edge, resulting in a 90% increase since I initiated coverage and 48% since my last report. In this report, I revisit the risks and opportunities for ISSC and update my stock price target.

The Investment Thesis For ISSC, The Risks And Opportunities

The investment case for ISSC is actually a very simple one; The company has significant growth opportunities driven by the legacy Honeywell product lines that it acquired. However, just stating that doesn't cover the full profile. There are also some pressures that should be understood as some of these pressures are transient and others may stick a bit longer.

ISSC is capitalizing on product lines that Honeywell no longer wants. That is not because the product lines are bad, but for Honeywell they simply may not make sense to continue operating. Programs have a certain economic life, more often than not with a revenue peak. After that peak occurs, it becomes more interesting to focus on other programs and get rid of the program altogether. Some programs also may simply no longer fit the scope or margin profile. ISSC aims to capitalize on those programs.

In July 2023, ISSC announced the acquisition of exclusive licenses and assets for inertial, communication and navigation lines from Honeywell. That is what can be seen as the foundational deal with Honeywell. A year later, the company acquired additional licenses for Communication & Navigation product lines adding exclusive rights to manufacture, repair and upgrade. This fully completed the acquisition of the inertial, communication and navigation product lines.

In October, a new agreement was announced for display generations and flight control computers and this brought the F-16 program in ISSC's scope.

The major risk that the company has faced centers on margins and integration. As long as integration is not fully completed, the efficiency is somewhat lower. Furthermore, we do note that margin pressures also occurs when the production line transitions from Honeywell to ISSC's facilities. As Honeywell does have commitments to customers, upon acquisition it meant that ISSC had to operate the Honeywell lines while also setting up their own production lines. Simply speaking it brings duplicate costs including labor and higher asset base for depreciation and some inventory build for a smooth transition. The integration and duplicate pressures are transient. One item that may be more sticky is that ISSC has to honor the agreements Honeywell has with customers. That means that it also has to follow the agreed pricing schedule. Under the agreement that Honeywell has with its customers that may result in margins substantially below the margins that ISSC is used to and apart from margin lift from efficiency, the margins have little room to grow until the contract reaches a point where it can be repriced. Once the F-16 program is fully integrated and excluding repricing, the margins should grow to the mid-40s.

The company does have some additional levers to boost margins. Currently, the company is tripling its capacity in its Exton plant. That allows for growing capacity and thus revenues, but also to insource more work and cut some cost away that would usually go to suppliers. The company may also decide to acquire other companies to become more vertically integrated.

Other tailwinds include reshoring of defense work and growing demand for avionics including a tailwind from the defense market.

F-16 Impact Shows In Q3 Earnings, Negative And Positive

The impact of the F-16 program showed quite clearly in the Q3 results; Sales increased $12.3 million to $24.1 million indicating 105% growth and that can be attributed to the addition of the F-16 line and that also makes it the biggest revenue platform for ISSC at this moment. At the same time, we do note that cost of sales rose 184% due to the integration effort and duplicate costs resulting in gross profits to increase "only" 36.6% with gross margins contracting from 53.4% to 35.6%. Operating income increased from $2 million to $3.5 million indicating margins of 14.6% compared to 17.3%. So, the margin pressure is clearly visible.

Based on product mix and completion of the F-16 integration, the company expects gross margins to reach the mid-40s. This also does show that as long as agreements are not being repriced to better align with ISSC's overhead cost allocation to the program, there is some margin pressure. At the same time, it does allow us to calculate what "clean" margins would have looked like in Q3 and those margins would have been around 24% and do show that while gross margins may be depressed as mid-40s margins are lower than the 53% margins seen before, the operating margins can be lifted substantially.

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

I previously saw upside of roughly 55-60 percent based on earnings in 2024. However, as time progresses the earnings, we pull into our target also evolves. Based on 2026 earnings, I put my price target at $21.51, indicating almost 79% upside. We also note that the company trades at a discount to its median trading EV/EBITDA and is substantially undervalued against peers. All of that provides additional support to the strong buy rating.

We note that EBITDA estimates have not substantially changed. For 2025, I revised down by 9.7% but up by 6.7% for 2027 while estimates for 2026 did not materially change. This means that total EBITDA change was negligible and the CAGR is 33.3%. Free cash flow expectations have been revised downward by 14.7% this year, 3% next year but up 3.7% in the year after for a total downward change of 4.6% but the CAGR continues to be strong at 62.7%. So, there is some execution risk on addressing the transient pressures, but we see high EBITDA growth opportunities and free cash flow growing almost at twice the EBITDA growth rate. I believe that it provides solid support for the strong buy rating.

Conclusion: ISSC Stock Remains Extremely Compelling

While ISSC is facing some transient margin pressure as well as some more sticky margin pressure until contract repricing kicks in, I do believe that the company remains extremely attractive as it has very high growth prospects while trading below the peer group and below its own median valuation. All of that shapes into a strong buy rating.

data.title: Corporate Profits: A Reading Without Rose-Tinted Glasses
data.description:
If you want to understand where we are in the cycle, skip the noise and follow profits. Corporate profits are the lifeblood of investment, hiring, and market returns. Crucially, linkage to the real economy is very tight. In the national accounts (NIPA), the BEA’s “profits from current production” (with inventory valuation and capital consumption adjustments) rose in Q2-2025, but only modestly: up $6.8 billion from Q1, and notably revised down by $58.7 billion from the prior estimate. That’s not the surge you’d expect if we were entering a new, powerful profit upswing. The correlation is unsurprising, given that economic activity generates the revenue to obtain corporate profits.

While the revision to the third estimate of real Q2 GDP growth increased to 3.8% annualized, all was not what it seemed. The reversal of the import surge in Q1 to get ahead of tariffs did the heavy lifting in Q2. More notably, consumer spending, the main driver of economic activity, showed continued weakness. Again, the linkage between PCE and corporate profits is critical, given that spending generates corporate revenues.

The point for investors is that while the economic growth number “looks” hot, the profits revision tells a quieter story about corporate income momentum. In other words, output accelerated, but profit growth didn’t follow in lockstep. That divergence matters for equity investors who ultimately get paid in earnings, not GDP. On a level basis, after-tax corporate profits (CPATAX) stood at roughly $3.26 trillion SAAR in Q2-2025, near the high end of the post-pandemic range but not breaking decisively higher. However, net profit margins have come under pressure, and economic growth has slowed. That “plateau with wiggles” profile of the last two years remains intact, and while margins remain elevated, when margins flatten as price multiples rise, future return math tends to get harder.

Zoom in on the listed companies, and you get a similar nuance. FactSet’s S&P 500 Q2-2025 dashboard shows blended earnings growth accelerating year-over-year with net profit margins around 12.3%, still above long-run norms and reflecting solid breadth of beats. Good news, but the market had already priced a lot of good news. However, the rest of the economy is not seeing the same growth. The deviation between large public and small private net operating surpluses is quite dramatic.

Regular readers of our work at RealInvestmentAdvice will remember we’ve been writing for years that profits and the economy move together over complete cycles and that revenue and profits don’t levitate indefinitely above economic capacity. The thread through my prior pieces, specifically on Kalecki’s profit identity, is the detachment of markets from fundamentals. The earnings-economy linkage, fiscal impulses, savings behavior, and trade balances can push profits temporarily above trend, but gravity eventually reasserts itself.

That lens is still helpful in 2025.

Profits, Prices, and Pay: How Inflation Filters Into Margins

The revival of an old debate followed the pandemic and its aftermath. Are corporate margins the cause of inflation or the result of it? As discussed in Corporate Greed Is Not The Cause Of Inflation, corporations are victims of inflation, not the cause.

“One simply has to reason through the claim to uncover the absurdity. If corporations can willy-nilly raise prices and enjoy “excessive” profits, why don’t they do it all the time? Did corporations suddenly get greedy in 2021? And why did the Federal Reserve spend a decade fretting about inflation being ‘too low’ as it struggled to hit its 2% target? Was there not enough corporate greed before coronavirus?” – Michael Maharrey

The European Central Bank (ECB) was one of several studies confirming our previous thesis. “Profit-led inflation” can emerge when factors constrain supply but demand remains high. Fed Chair Jerome Powell also noted such:

“The ongoing episode of high inflation initially emerged from a collision between very strong demand and pandemic-constrained supply. By the time the Federal Open Market Committee raised the policy rate in March 2022, it was clear that bringing down inflation would depend on both the unwinding of the unprecedented pandemic-related demand and supply distortions and on our tightening of monetary policy, which would slow the growth of aggregate demand, allowing supply time to catch up.”

In other words, basic economics states that if the supply/demand curve shifts, inflation will be the consequence if supply constricts.

While the “greedflation” narrative resonated with media pundits, corporations struggled with a supply shortage amid a stimulus-driven demand surge. However, that tailwind for profit margins is now gone. As the San Francisco Fed noted, markup fluctuations have not driven U.S. inflation in the post-pandemic disinflation phase. As supply chains healed and demand normalized, the contribution from markups cooled. Inflation’s path has increasingly reflected costs and supply/demand rebalancing rather than persistent profit-push. The nuance matters as it tells you whether margins will keep inflating prices or mean-revert as costs and demand fluctuate.

With inflation moderating but still somewhat sticky in services, maintaining profit margins is becoming more difficult. If the economy slows as demand slows, that difficulty will increase. Crucially, unit labor costs, the most essential recurring input, increased by just 1.0% in Q2. When price growth slows while labor-cost pressures ease, margins can hold up, but only if top-line growth remains decent. That’s the narrow path corporate America is walking today, as shown in a recent analysis by Albert Edwards at Societe Generale.

“Unit labour cost inflation, which economists regard as the key source of cost push inflation, has slowed to below 1%, suggesting that the sharp fall in NFCB (non-financial corporate business) inflation is not anomalous.”

Let’s pull the macro and micro together. Corporate profit margins are still very elevated versus history. While Q2 earnings did fine, slowing economic growth is a risk. Furthermore, as inflation gravitates toward the Fed’s target range and productivity rebounds, the “easy” boost to margins from price hikes fades. Profits depend more on real demand growth, productivity gains, fiscal impulse, and mix, not just pricing power. That’s consistent with the Kalecki framework. Government “dis-saving” (deficits), household saving behavior, net investment, and trade flows explain the macro profit pool.

“The Kalecki Profit Equation clearly explains that while debts and deficits erode economic growth and are deflationary through the diversion of capital from productive investment, a reversal of deficit spending suggests risk for investors. Valuations are high, partly because investors assume elevated profit margins will persist. However, the cumulative change of the inflation-adjusted price of the market significantly exceeds the profits being generated. Previous such deviations have not ended well for investors, which is what the Kalecki equation suggests.”

If deficits shrink and households retrench, profit margins become harder to defend, no matter how clever the pricing strategy.

Valuations, Sentiment, and Profits

Now to the uncomfortable bit. Valuations and sentiment have been running ahead of the actual improvement in the profit base. As discussed in the “Bull vs Bear Case,” valuations are already elevated. Forward P/E for the S&P 500 sits at 22.5x earnings, with trailing earnings at 25x. UBS notes that such readings are among the top 5% since 1985.

Furthermore, high valuations mean expectations are high and reflect investor sentiment. The risk, of course, is that if earnings disappoint, then forward valuations (expectations) must be recalculated, and currently, the margin for error is slim at best. Notably, given that earnings are derived from actual economic activity, the current gap between the annual change in earnings and GDP is notable. The long historical correlation between the two suggests that a higher risk to investors may be present more than realized.

Sentiment says the same thing. Investor sentiment readings have spent much of Q2 and Q3 above their long-term average, and “greed” metrics have frequently leaned hot, even as breadth narrows to a handful of mega-caps. When optimism, narrow leadership, and premium valuations line up, the market becomes more dependent on flawless execution from profits. Revisions don’t need to be disastrous to cause price air pockets; they only need to be “less great.”

Notably, some of this cycle’s EPS strength is still financial engineering rather than organic profit growth. Corporate buybacks remain enormous and on pace to exceed $1 trillion in 2025. While they reduce share counts to lift per-share earnings, it also depletes capital that could have been used for more productive purposes. That’s not a moral judgment; it’s simple arithmetic. But it does mean EPS can look stronger than underlying profits, which matters when investors pay a premium multiple for that EPS.

This is why, at RIA, we’ve kept hammering on the detachment theme in 2025. When markets run far ahead of the profits-GDP complex, future returns compress and the margin for error shrinks. That doesn’t mean an imminent crash, as bulls don’t die of old age, but it does mean risk-adjusted returns deteriorate when price outruns earnings power.

Conclusion: The Investor’s Risk Map From Here

Here are the four takeaways for investors from this discussion.

  • Profits are fine, not fabulous. The national accounts show profits rising slightly but being revised lower; S&P 500 margins remain high but not accelerating. That constellation is “good enough” for a range-bound market, but fragile if growth cools or if a sector with heavy index weight wobbles.
  • The inflation tailwind for margins is fading. Disinflation plus a downshift in unit labor cost growth is constructive for margins, but it also takes away the easy price-pass-through that boosted 2021–22 profitability. From here, real demand and productivity have to carry the baton. Revenue growth will test today’s margins if consumer spending slows, because of resumed student loan payments, tighter credit, or slower job gains.
  • Valuation risk is no longer theoretical. With forward P/E north of ~22× and sentiment often leaning greedy, the market is paying for growth, durability, and AI-era productivity gains to materialize broadly. That can work if the profits/GDP engine follows through. But it also means negative EPS revisions, narrower breadth, or even “less great” guidance can trigger outsized drawdowns.
  • Financial engineering can’t do all the lifting. Buybacks will keep underpinning EPS, but they don’t expand the economy-wide profit pie. When insiders sell aggressively into repurchase programs, the optics (EPS) can look better than the underlying economics (aggregate profits), especially if the fiscal impulse fades in 2026. That’s a classic setup for multiple compression even without a profit recession.

The economy is growing, profits are okay, but risk is ahead as inflation cools, and labor cost pressure eases. That mix can support near-term stability, but not complacency. If you’ve benefited from this year’s rally, think in terms of risk-budgeting: where are your exposures most tethered to unchallenged margin assumptions, optimistic revisions, and valuation premia? That’s where small disappointments can have a significant price impact. The playbook that’s worked for us all year remains intact: trim extensions, add on weakness, keep duration and factor exposures diversified, and let the data lead.

In markets, corporate profits write the checks. Make sure your portfolio is aligned with the part of the story that’s actually funding the narrative.
