data.title: TDF: Chinese Economy Has Some Headwinds, But This Fund Is Decent
data.description:
The Templeton Dragon Fund (NYSE:TDF) is a somewhat underfollowed and little-known closed-end fund that provides investors with an easy way to gain exposure to China via an actively managed portfolio. Active management could be an advantage when investing in a place such as China, as many investors do not completely trust the nation or the financials that some of the companies in the country put out. It could be advantageous to have a second set of eyes on your assets in that country as a way to protect against potential problems.

It is arguable how much of an advantage the active management of this fund has truly provided, though. Over the past ten years, investors in the Templeton Dragon Fund have realized a 60.65% total return, which is slightly worse than the total return of the MSCI China Index (MCHI) over the same period:

While this is not the best performance over the long-term, we can see that there were times in which TDF was outperforming the MSCI China Index by a substantial margin. For example, back during the market mania of 2020 and 2021, this fund was significantly outperforming the index. However, it also fell more than the index in the ensuing collapse of 2022, and it has suffered somewhat in recent years due to China keeping its COVID-era lockdowns active for longer than most other countries and struggling ever since its reopening to get its economy back to where it once was. In other words, what we see here is that this fund has been much more volatile than the index, which is actually somewhat surprising for a fund that does not employ leverage.

However, it is worth noting that it has been necessary to reinvest the distributions that this fund pays out in order to get the best results out of it. This is because, if we look merely at the fund’s share price movements, it has underperformed the index by a substantial margin over the past ten years:

As we can see here, TDF’s share price has declined by 40.90% whereas the MSCI China Index is actually up about 35.18% over the trailing ten-year period. While this may reduce the fund’s appeal in the eyes of potential investors, we should keep in mind the reason for it. In short, the Templeton Dragon Fund pays out all of its profits to the investors, including realized gains, whereas the index fund rarely realizes gains. This is one reason why the Templeton Dragon Fund consistently boasts a higher yield than does the MSCI China Index. As of right now, TDF yields 3.38% whereas MCHI has a more modest 2.16% yield. The current yield of this fund, moreover, is actually lower than it has historically possessed. If we take a look at the fund’s distribution history, we can see that its quarterly distributions over the 2020 to 2022 period were substantially higher than the distributions that it pays today:

As we can see, the fund’s current distribution is $0.10 per quarter, which works out to only $0.40 per year. However, back in the 2020 to 2022 period, the fund was regularly paying out distributions that were well in excess of twice that per share. Admittedly, the fact that the fund is now paying a stable quarterly distribution might be somewhat more attractive to income-seeking investors who want a more regular and stable payment schedule. However, the fund still does not have the monthly payment schedule of more income-oriented funds, and it is best to consider this fund as a wealth-building fund rather than as a great vehicle for income. After all, as we just saw, anyone who took the distributions that this fund has paid out over time and spent them has lost a great deal of their initial principal.

Peer Distribution Comparison

As we just saw, the current 3.38% yield of the Templeton Dragon Fund is significantly higher than the current 2.16% yield of the MSCI China Index. It is also far higher than the yields of most domestic and global broad-market equity indices:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

S&P 500 Index (SPY)

1.08%

NASDAQ-100 Index (QQQ)

0.46%

Russell 2000 Index (IWM)

0.98%

MSCI World Index (URTH)

1.29%

MSCI All-Countries World Index (ACWI)

1.45%

MSCI All-Countries World ex-U.S. Index (ACWX)

2.44%

MSCI Emerging Markets Index (EEM)

2.16%

The highest-yielding of these indices is, perhaps surprisingly, the one that covers all of the world’s equity markets except for the United States. It is not surprising that this index would have a higher yield than the ones that include the United States simply because of the fact that the United States has one of the most richly valued equity markets in the world. This is immediately apparent when we consider that the United States accounts for 64.36% of the MSCI All-Countries World Index:

The United States does not account for anywhere close to 64.36% of the world’s total economic output. According to the International Monetary Fund, the total gross domestic product of all 194 countries in the world was $110.06 trillion in nominal terms in 2024. The gross domestic product of the United States was estimated at $29.1849 trillion in 2024:

That works out to be 26.52% of the total world’s economic output. The only conclusion that we can draw from this is that the United States is substantially overvalued relative to its actual contribution to the global economy. As a general rule, whenever valuations go up, yields go down due to the simple fact that, unlike common stock valuations, dividend payments have to be supported by actual cash flow. Thus, due to the richly valued market in the United States right now, its dividend yields are naturally lower.

The thing that may be surprising is that the yield of the MSCI Emerging Markets Index is as low as it is. Emerging markets have been almost neglected as an investment destination for most of the past fifteen years. This chart shows the MSCI Emerging Markets Index against the S&P 500 Index (SP500) over the period from January 1, 2010, through October 8, 2025:

As we can see, the S&P 500 Index delivered a whopping 486.54% gain over the period, whereas the MSCI Emerging Markets Index only delivered 26.13%. This means that investors were almost completely ignoring emerging markets over the entire fifteen-year period. However, many of the countries in the index deliver significantly stronger gross domestic product growth than the United States. Here are the twelve largest countries in the index:

According to the IMF, these countries will grow their gross domestic products at the following rates in 2025:

Country

Gross Domestic Product Growth Rate

China

4.0%

Taiwan

2.9%

India

6.2%

1.0%

2.0%

1.0%

Saudi Arabia

3.0%

Mexico

-0.3%

United Arab Emirates

4.0%

Malaysia

4.1%

Indonesia

4.7%

Thailand

1.8%

The International Monetary Fund puts its estimate for the United States at 1.8% for the 2025 year. As we can see, only four of the twelve countries shown have a lower or equal growth rate than the United States. Eight of them have higher projected growth rates. This is hardly a new occurrence, either, as several of these markets have been growing at a more rapid pace than the United States for years. For example, China’s gross domestic product was $6.087 trillion in 2010. It was $17.795 trillion in 2023, a 192.34% increase. The United States increased its gross domestic product by 84.20% over the same period. We see the same thing for India (112.89% increase) and Indonesia (84.22% increase). While it is true that several of the countries shown above did actually grow their gross domestic products at a lower compound annual growth rate than the United States, nearly all of them delivered better than a 26.13% increase in economic output that is implied by the performance of the emerging market index. Thus, by this measure, many emerging markets are actually cheaper than they were fifteen years ago in relation to their economic output. We might expect that this apparent improvement in valuation would cause the yield of the MSCI Emerging Market Index to be higher relative to the international developed markets than it actually is. This could be a sign that companies in these emerging market nations are retaining their capital in order to fund expansion rather than distributing it to the investors via dividends. If this is indeed the case, it could lead to further economic growth. If this economic growth occurs in China, as the International Monetary Fund expects will be the case, then it could be beneficial to the owners of the Templeton Dragon Fund.

The current 3.38% yield of the Templeton Dragon Fund is not particularly attractive when compared to several of the fund’s peers. We can see this in the following chart:

Fund Name

One interesting thing that we note here is that the majority of these funds are single-country funds that invest only in one country located in the Asia-Pacific region. This is probably a good thing for investors, as many of these countries have very different governments, economic growth rates, and economic fundamentals. The fact that investors can pick and choose among the countries in which they wish to invest themselves is likely for the best, given this situation, as it ensures that they have maximum control over their risk profiles.

Given this single-country situation, the funds that are closest to TDF in terms of being direct peers are CHN and CAF. After all, the Templeton Dragon Fund currently has 88.43% of its assets invested in mainland China and 7.51% invested in the special administrative region of Hong Kong:

TDF has the highest yield of any of the China-specific funds shown in the peer grouping. However, it is certainly not the highest-yielding among all of the closed-end funds that invest in countries that are located in the Asia-Pacific region.

One thing that we should note, however, is that few of the peer funds were actually designed for income-seeking investors who are looking for a relatively safe and stable source of income to pay their bills. Most of the funds shown above have variable distributions that depend heavily on the dividend income and capital gains that a fund achieves during a given period. This is fine for someone who wants to simply buy a fund and reinvest the distributions. However, investors who actually want to earn some spending money will probably be happier investing in something with a monthly distribution that does not fluctuate too heavily.

About The Fund

The website for the Templeton Dragon Fund states that the fund has the primary objective of providing its investors with long-term capital appreciation. This makes sense for an equity fund, and indeed, the fund’s most recent semi-annual report makes it very clear that this is an equity fund. This document provides the following asset allocation as of June 30, 2025:

Asset Type

% of Net Assets

Common Stocks

99.6%

Escrows and Litigation Trusts

0.0%

Money Market Funds

0.0%

Investments from Cash Collateral Received for Loaned Securities

0.9%

Although the above chart states that the fund had a 0.0% allocation to the “Escrows and Litigation Trusts” category and the “Money Market Funds” category, this does not mean that the fund has no money invested in those things. The semi-annual report states that as of June 30, 2025, the Templeton Dragon Fund had $83,711 invested in Escrows and Litigation Trusts, and another $9,606 invested in money market funds. Those two amounts are only a small fraction of the $293,501,325 that the fund had in net assets as of the same date, which is why the chart states that the fund has no exposure to those categories when it actually does.

The strategy that the fund states on its website is somewhat confusing. From the website:

The fund seeks long-term capital appreciation by investing at least 45% of its total assets in the equity securities of “China companies,” as defined in the prospectus.

For some reason, this fund makes it incredibly difficult to even find its prospectus. The SEC has four documents listed for this fund on Form 425 (Prospectuses and communications) and not one of them provides a definition for the term “China companies.” Furthermore, the annual reports do not include a definition of this term. This is among the only funds that I have ever seen that do not provide a downloadable prospectus on their websites. Based on the assets in which this fund is invested, the definition appears to be companies that are domiciled in mainland China or Hong Kong, and possibly Taiwan. The semi-annual report makes the following statement:

There are special risks associated with investments in China, Hong Kong and Taiwan,

This suggests that the fund might include some Taiwan companies from time to time, although it does not currently have any exposure to that location. This is somewhat surprising since most funds that have the ability to invest in Taiwanese companies include a position in Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM), given that stock’s enormous outperformance over the past year. As we can see here, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing is up a whopping 61.30% over the past twelve months, substantially outperforming the S&P 500 Index:

That level of outperformance would ordinarily entice fund managers to buy into the company in order to get some profits for the fund’s shareholders. The fact that this fund does not have a position in this company could suggest that it is, in fact, not willing or able to invest in Taiwan. This is not necessarily a problem, so long as investors know what they are getting into, it would just be nice if the fund posted a prospectus on its website that specifically states what countries it can and cannot invest in.

In addition to this, the fund states that it only needs to invest 45% of its assets into “China companies.” It currently has significantly above that level, but the fact that it only states that it has to have 45% invested in “China companies” gives it the ability to reduce its holdings significantly. This poses the question of where it will invest the remainder of its assets if it were to drop down to the minimum required level. The fund’s documentation (at least the documentation that is easily discoverable from the fund’s website and the SEC) does not answer that question.

In short, I do not like the lack of transparency here. Franklin Templeton usually does a pretty good job at providing investors with all of the documentation that they need to make an informed decision about its funds, but for some reason, that is not the case with this one. It might be advisable to steer clear of it for now until it provides a more concrete description of its strategy.

The website for the Templeton Dragon Fund does not provide a listing of its largest holdings. However, it does provide a listing of the fund’s holdings by industry sector, and we can see from this that the fund is reasonably well-diversified:

The benchmark index that is being used here is the MSCI China Index, which is the same one that is being tracked by the MCHI ETF. We can see that TDF does have a lot of similarities to the index, as its weightings in most categories are pretty similar to the index. However, this fund is severely underweight semiconductors, energy, and real estate relative to the index.

It is likely somewhat of a smart move for TDF to be underweight the real estate sector compared to the MSCI China Index. China has been suffering a real estate crisis for some time now, and as an Australian news service reported a few days ago, house prices in China are down about 20% from their peak:

The analyst at Australia’s News.com blames this on overbuilding, as people rushed from villages in China to the big cities in search of jobs. That flow has now apparently reversed, which has reduced the demand for urban apartments and caused some of the major property development companies in China to go bankrupt. From the article:

In the West, this would turn into a financial crisis, like we saw in 2008 in America. Unpaid debts lead to doubts about who is solvent, then lending dries up, and the whole economic system grinds to a halt. Boom.

China has not yet suffered from a financial crisis akin to the one that America suffered fifteen years ago, partly due to the Chinese government attempting to manage it by boosting its manufacturing sector. In effect, the country is attempting to export its way to prosperity. It is unclear whether or not this will be successful, but the simple fact that this fund is largely avoiding the real estate sector is a pretty good sign, as it suggests that the fund’s management is trying to overweight those sectors that are still earning profits.

Unfortunately for China, it does not have much of a consumption market at home. According to CNBC, more than 60% of Chinese consumers have expressed a market preference for saving instead of spending. This is not necessarily a bad thing, since domestic savings provide capital for businesses. The problem arises if export markets, including the United States and Europe, are no longer willing to purchase Chinese-made goods. Numerous headlines have come out over the past ten months mentioning the attempts of the current administration to reduce America’s trade deficit with China, and it appears that it has been having success at that. CNBC states that the June 2025 U.S. trade deficit with China shrank to its lowest level in 21 years. The European Union has also expressed a desire to reduce its trade deficit with China. Given the reluctance of Chinese consumers to purchase goods, this could be difficult to accomplish without the European Union restricting its imports from China. Thus, China might be in a situation where its export-driven economic model might be in jeopardy. It needs to boost domestic consumption, as it does have a sizable population that could purchase the things that its factories produce, but the country has been having trouble doing that.

Distribution Analysis

The Templeton Dragon Fund recently adopted a managed distribution policy. From the semi-annual report:

Effective March 1, 2025, the Board has implemented a new managed distribution plan whereby the Fund will distribute a level distribution amount to shareholders. The Fund intends to make quarterly distributions to shareholders at the fixed rate of $0.10 per share. Management will generally distribute amounts necessary to satisfy the Fund’s plan and the requirements prescribed by excise tax rules and Subchapter M of the Internal Revenue Code.

Prior to this, the fund made an annual distribution that was roughly equivalent to its investment profits. This is why it varied so much from year to year. The new policy has the fund distributing $0.10 per share quarterly, and presumably it will distribute any investment profits that it has in excess of this amount via a special distribution at the end of the year to avoid excise tax penalties. Of course, there is no guarantee that the fund will have excess investment profits to distribute in any given year.

Of course, we should still have a look at the fund’s finances in order to see how easily it can afford the distribution that it currently pays out. After all, we still don’t want a situation where the fund is distributing more than it can really afford. For the purposes of our analysis, we will consult the semi-annual report for the six-month period that ended on June 30, 2025. A link to this document was provided earlier in this article.

For the six-month period that ended on June 30, 2025, the Templeton Dragon Fund reported a net investment income of $2,104,991. That was not sufficient to cover the $5,062,422 that the fund paid out in distributions over the same period.

Fortunately, this fund was easily able to cover the difference through capital gains. For the six-month period that ended on June 30, 2025, TDF reported net realized gains of $1,206,999, and it had an additional $33,104,916 net unrealized gains. Overall, the fund had investment profits of $36,416,906 for the period:

This was easily enough to cover the distributions that it paid out. However, the fund did have to rely on unrealized gains to achieve full coverage of the distribution. This can be problematic, given that unrealized gains can be erased by market weakness. Fortunately, that does not appear to be the case for this fund right now. This chart shows the fund’s net asset value since the closing date of its semi-annual report:

As we can see, the fund’s net asset value per share is up 18.69% since the June 30, 2025, closing date of its most recent financial report. This means that the fund was able to cover the distributions that it paid out last month as well as hold onto its gains. Thus, for now, it does appear that this fund is having no difficulty covering its distributions. As the fund appears to be relying on unrealized gains, though, it would be wise for investors to keep a close eye on this fund’s net asset value in order to ensure that it does not lose its unrealized gains before it sells the stocks and takes the money.

Valuation

Shares of TDF are currently trading at a 15.38% discount on net asset value. This is a wider discount than the 14.46% discount that the shares have had on average over the past month.

Historically, shares of the Templeton Dragon Fund have traded at fairly large discounts on net asset value. Over the past three years, the fund’s shares have traded at prices ranging from a 7.83% discount to an 18.95% discount on net asset value. The average price over the period was a 14.92% discount, which is slightly more expensive than what the fund’s shares trade at today.

Here is how the current price of the Templeton Dragon Fund compares with its peers:

Fund Name

Premium/(Discount)

Templeton Dragon Fund

(15.38%)

The China Fund

(3.74%)

Japan Smaller Capitalization Fund

(9.60%)

The Korea Fund

(14.08%)

(14.52%)

Morgan Stanley India Investment Fund

(11.11%)

The Taiwan Fund

(14.66%)

The India Fund

(5.38%)

Voya Asia Pacific High Dividend Equity Income Fund

(6.00%)

abrdn Japan Equity Fund

(0.72%)

One thing that we immediately notice here is that all of the peer funds are trading at prices that are below their net asset values. Thus, none of these funds appear to be especially overvalued right now. The cheapest of these funds is TDF, though, so we can clearly see that the Templeton Dragon Fund is not overvalued compared to its peer group. When we also consider that the fund is trading at a price that is below its historical average, we can draw the conclusion that the current price is reasonable as an entry point.

TDF Expense Ratio

The fund’s website states that the Templeton Dragon Fund has an expense ratio of 1.36%:

This fund does not employ leverage, so it has no leverage-related expenses.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the Templeton Dragon Fund is a China-focused closed-end fund that perhaps does not provide as much transparency as investors might like. If we can look past that, however, the fund offers a fairly well-diversified portfolio of Chinese and Hong Kong-based companies and boasts a respectable 3.38% dividend yield. The fund is not particularly expensive, as it trades at a massive discount to the underlying value of its assets, and the aggregate trailing price-to-earnings ratio of its portfolio is 19.72, which is a bit lower than most American companies. The fund recently converted to a quarterly distribution schedule, which might help reduce the discount on net asset value. Overall, this fund might be worth considering if you want exposure to China, but China itself does have some economic struggles to overcome at the moment.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
